headline: J.C. Collins Lynched in Mondak, 1913
body:
The raw wind of April, still carrying the bite of winter, whipped across the Missouri River bottoms near Mondak in 1913. It was a place where men clawed at the earth, building a bridge that would bind the wild lands, and where the law often bent to the will of the brutal frontier. J.C. Collins, a man forged in hardship and shadowed by a past of trouble, found himself caught in the maelstrom. Thirty-four years had etched a hard life onto his frame, a life spent running from the consequences of gambling, drink, and the quick, violent temper that had followed him from Arkansas to Iowa, from Omaha to Minot. He was a colored man, and in those days, the odds were always stacked against such a one.

Mondak itself was a crucible, a boomtown straddling the Montana-North Dakota line, fueled by whiskey and the transient dreams of men. It was a place where saloons outnumbered churches, and the rough justice of the prairie often outpaced the slow grind of the courts. Collins, a construction worker on the new lift bridge, had sought a fresh start, or at least a place where his past might be swallowed by the vastness. But trouble, like a hungry wolf, always found his scent. It began with a woman, the wife of a new shack owner, whom Collins had struck in a fit of rage after refusing to vacate a shared dwelling. The husband, a timid sort, carried his grievance to the law.

Sheriff Thomas Courtney of Sheridan County, a man tasked with bringing order to this untamed corner, arrived on April 4th with Deputy Richard Burmeister. They brought with them a newly deputized citizen, a man named Wilson, who claimed to know the layout of the sprawling man camp. Wilson entered the construction office, warrant in hand, while Courtney and Burmeister waited outside, leaning against their automobile, alert but perhaps underestimating the raw desperation of the man they sought. The air crackled with unspoken tension, for many men in Mondak had come to escape the law, and the arrival of the sheriff was a spectacle.

Collins, however, was no ordinary quarry. He emerged from the office, a short-barrel revolver already in his grasp, startling Sheriff Courtney. The lawman reached for his own weapon, but the movement was too slow, the moment too brief. Two shots tore through the air, and Sheriff Courtney fell, dead before he hit the muddy ground. Deputy Burmeister, a loyal man, drew his pistol, but Collins, now armed with Wilson's automatic as well, was quicker. Five slugs ripped into Burmeister's flesh—shoulder, liver, arms, and legs. The deputy collapsed, his lifeblood staining the raw earth.

They rushed Burmeister to Williston by special train, a desperate race against time at forty miles an hour. But the wounds were too grievous. Less than two hours later, he breathed his last, his final words a testament to the brutal loyalty of the frontier: "I supposed it was all over with me. I feel sorry for my wife and children. How is Sheriff Cortney? I hope he did not get killed." Collins, a hunted beast, fled into the thick brush along the Missouri River, seeking the meager cover of the woods. But the prairie had its own reckoning. He was apprehended, dragged from the flimsy jail, and delivered to the swift, merciless judgment of a mob. They strung him from a power pole, a grim silhouette against the vast Montana sky, and riddled his body with bullets. The law, in Mondak, had been broken, and then brutally remade, leaving behind only the stark memory of violence and the chilling silence of a life extinguished.
location: Mondak, Roosevelt County (near Plentywood, Sheridan County)
